title: Auditing your household waste stream
subtitle: What a one-week waste audit tells you and how to run one
excerpt: A household waste audit is the fastest way to find where waste can be reduced. Most households have no clear picture of what they throw away or why. One week of sorting and weighing gives a breakdown by material type: food, packaging, paper, glass, metal, textiles. The audit is the starting point for any serious reduction effort.
sourceNotes: WRAP household food and drink waste in the UK (2021). Defra UK household waste composition data (2022). Zero Waste Europe household audit methodology. WRAP local authority recycling quality guide.

content.text: A UK household generates about 400 to 500 kg of waste per year on average. Of this, 30 to 40 percent is food waste, 20 to 25 percent is packaging, 15 to 20 percent is paper and card, and the rest is glass, metal, textiles, and miscellaneous. The split differs by household, which is why the audit matters: your specific breakdown determines where reducing is easiest.
How to run a one-week audit

Choose a typical week (not a holiday week or a week with guests, as this skews food and packaging waste). Label four bags or boxes: General waste, Recyclable packaging, Food waste, and Other (textiles, small electricals, batteries).

Every item that leaves the kitchen or living space goes into one of the four categories before it enters the bin. Do not count the recycling bin separately: audit everything that the household generates.
At the end of each day, weigh each category bag on a kitchen scale and note the weight. Reset the bags for the next day.
At the end of the week, total each category. Express as kilograms per person per week.
What to look for in the results
Food waste above 1 kg per person per week: higher than the UK average of 0.9 kg. Look at what types of food are in the bag: vegetables going soft, bread, leftovers, or packaged food past its date.
Recyclable packaging in the general waste bag: items that could have gone in the recycling bin but did not. This is often soft plastics, polystyrene, or items where the household was unsure.
High general waste: if more than 40 percent goes to the landfill bag, recycling confusion or poor food waste habits is the likely cause.
High food waste: meal planning and a kitchen caddy with daily composting are the most effective fixes.
Recycling confusion: check the local council's recycling guide online. Most councils list exactly what goes in each bin by material type.

High packaging waste: identify the three or four product types generating the most packaging. Switching to a loose or bulk alternative for those items typically reduces packaging weight by 30 to 50 percent for that category.

Repeat the audit three months after making changes. The comparison shows what worked. A household that consistently audits and adjusts typically reduces general waste by 25 to 35 percent within a year, without significant changes to shopping habits.
